articles.title: Liam and Noel Gallagher's First Joint Interview in 25 Years Is Coming to Disney+
articles.summary: An untitled Oasis documentary covering the 2025 Live '25 reunion tour is heading to IMAX theaters on September 11 before streaming on Disney+ and Hulu later in 2026. The film includes the first joint interview Liam and Noel Gallagher have given together in over 25 years. It is created by Peaky Blinders writer Steven Knight and directed by the duo behind Shut Up and Play the Hits, Dylan Southern and Will Lovelace.
articles.content:
If you ever needed proof that miracles happen, here it is. Liam and Noel Gallagher, the two brothers who spent the better part of two decades refusing to be in the same room, are sitting down for a joint interview together. And it is all going to be on Disney+.

The untitled Oasis documentary will be released in IMAX and other theaters for a limited theatrical engagement on September 11, and it will then stream on Disney+ internationally and on Disney+ and Hulu in the US later this year. The feature documentary charts Liam and Noel Gallagher's reunion tour Oasis Live '25, described as one of the most anticipated rock and roll comebacks of our time, and captures the experience and emotions of the band and their fans across the world. It includes rehearsal, backstage, and onstage access, as well as the first joint interviews with Noel and Liam in over 25 years.

Steven Knight, the creator and producer known for Peaky Blinders, is attached to the film, while Dylan Southern and Will Lovelace, best known for music documentaries Shut Up and Play the Hits and Meet Me in the Bathroom, are directing. The film comes from Disney, Magna Studios, and Sony Music Vision. Rather than a straight concert film, it features behind-the-scenes footage of the reunion, including access to rehearsals and performances.

Prior to Oasis reuniting for the massive world tour last year, the brothers were barely on speaking terms, let alone ready to sit for an interview together. Their 1995 interview "Wibbling Rivalry" is part of the band's lore, and a whole lot of bets did not cash last year when Liam and Noel managed to complete the 2025 tour without breaking up Oasis again before it was over. The fact that they sat down together and talked is, by itself, a headline.

The film aims to move beyond standard tour documentary fare, framing the 2025 world tour as a larger cultural moment, and will explore the emotional impact of the band's music on audiences across generations, alongside the on-stage and off-stage dynamics of the famously volatile siblings. Steven Knight summed it up well. "I genuinely cannot wait for the world to see this film. I believe it captures the spirit and emotion of a global cultural moment and does justice to the wit and genius of two exceptional people," Knight said. "I wanted to tell the story of the brothers and the band, but just as important, the story of the fans whose lives the music has touched and sometimes changed forever."
keyPoints: An untitled Oasis reunion documentary is coming to IMAX theaters on September 11, 2026
It will then stream on Disney+ internationally and on both Disney+ and Hulu in the US
The film features the first joint Liam and Noel Gallagher interview in over 25 years
Peaky Blinders creator Steven Knight produced it; Dylan Southern and Will Lovelace directed
The documentary covers the Oasis Live '25 tour with backstage, rehearsal, and onstage access

articles.title: Cloudflare Cuts 1,100 Jobs and Blames AI While Revenue Hits Record
articles.summary: Cloudflare announced its first ever mass layoff on May 7, 2026, cutting approximately 1,100 employees — about 20% of its entire workforce — and CEO Matthew Prince says AI is the reason. The news landed the same day Cloudflare reported record quarterly revenue of $639.8 million, a 34% year-over-year jump. Prince's argument: AI made those roles unnecessary, not the balance sheet.
articles.content:
Cloudflare has never done anything like this before. Cloudflare announced it was cutting its workforce by approximately 20%, which equates to 1,100 people, as part of its first quarter 2026 earnings report. "We've never done something like this in Cloudflare's history," co-founder and CEO Matthew Prince said on the quarterly conference call, marking the first mass layoff in the company's 16-year history. The cuts hit every department and every region. The company is cutting people from all teams and geographies except for salespeople who carry revenue quotas, CFO Thomas Seifert detailed on the call.

Here's what makes this story so striking. The news of the layoffs came as the company reported quarterly revenue of $639.8 million, up 34% year over year and the highest single quarter in the company's history. Cloudflare is not a company in trouble. It is a company actively choosing to shrink its headcount while growing its top line. Prince's explanation is direct: AI did it. "Internally, the tipping point was last November. At that point, across our teams, we began to see massive productivity gains, team members that were two, 10, even 100 times more productive than they had been before. It was like going from a manual to an electric screwdriver," he described.

"Cloudflare's usage of AI has increased by more than 600% in the last three months alone," Prince added. That explosion in internal AI use is what drove the headcount math. Highly productive AI-powered workers simply need fewer support staff around them. "Employees across the company, from engineering to HR to finance and marketing, run thousands of AI agent sessions every day to get their work done." As a result, Prince argued, these highly productive AI-driven employees need fewer support staff. "A lot of the supportive people behind them, those are not going to be the roles that move companies forward," Prince said.

When asked by an analyst on the call why the company needed to cut so deeply after such a good quarter, Prince said, "Just because you're fit doesn't mean you can't get fitter." He also pushed back on the idea that this is a one-way door. Prince says that Cloudflare "will continue to hire people, and we'll continue to invest in them because the people that are embracing these tools are just so much more productive than we'd ever seen before. I would guess that in 2027 we'll have more employees than we did at any point in 2026."

Cloudflare joined a growing list of tech companies — including Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon — that have reported increased revenue alongside massive layoffs, attributing both trends to their use of AI. The pattern Prince described — deploying AI gains as justification for workforce reductions even during a period of strong revenue growth — is fast becoming a familiar script across the tech industry. Whether it reflects true structural transformation or acts as convenient cover for cost discipline is a question that investors and employees will be wrestling with for some time to come. One thing is certain: the market was not impressed. Despite posting stronger-than-expected first-quarter results, the company's shares dropped nearly 19% in extended trading. Strong numbers, record revenue, and a clear AI strategy — and Wall Street still punished the stock. That tells you how uncomfortable this new playbook still makes people.
keyPoints: Cloudflare cut 1,100 employees — about 20% of its workforce — in the company's first ever mass layoff
CEO Matthew Prince blames AI efficiency gains, not financial pressure, for making the roles obsolete
Cloudflare's AI usage surged 600% in just the last three months before the announcement
Q1 2026 revenue hit a record $639.8 million, up 34% year over year — the same quarter the layoffs were announced
Cloudflare stock still dropped nearly 19% in after-hours trading despite the record results

articles.title: Mike D Drops "Switch Up," First Beastie Boys Music in 15 Years
articles.summary: Mike D has released "Switch Up," his debut solo single and the first new music from any Beastie Boys member since the group's 2011 album Hot Sauce Committee Part Two. The track was born out of home studio sessions with his sons Skyler and Davis Diamond and their band Very Nice Person, and it premiered live at a sold-out show at Los Angeles' Plaza Nightclub on May 7. It is a genre-blurring mix of hip-hop, funk, drum and bass, and rock that signals Mike D is very much back.
articles.content:
Fifteen years is a long time to stay quiet. But Mike D just broke the silence in the best way possible, dropping "Switch Up" on May 8, 2026, his first-ever solo single and the first new music from any member of the Beastie Boys since the group released Hot Sauce Committee Part Two back in 2011.

To understand why this matters, a little context helps. The Beastie Boys never officially broke up. They went on indefinite hiatus after founding member Adam "MCA" Yauch died of cancer in May 2012. Since then, Mike D and fellow surviving member Ad-Rock have been largely quiet on the music front, releasing the memoir Beastie Boys Book in 2018 and a Spike Jonze-directed documentary in 2020, but no new songs. In March 2026, the two put out a deluxe reissue of To the 5 Boroughs on triple-vinyl, but original material? Nothing. Until now.

"Switch Up" started as what the press release describes as "carefree, low-pressure experiments" in Mike D's home studio with his sons Davis and Skyler Diamond, who make music together as the indie-dance duo Very Nice Person. Those sessions grew into something bigger, pulling in additional collaborators and recording locations. The single was co-produced by Very Nice Person and Carter Lang, and mixed by Derek "MixedByAli" Ali at No Name Studios. Sonically, it is a genre-hopper: funky breakbeats, late-90s drum and bass energy, squiggly keyboards, rock guitars, and Mike D's forever-familiar voice riding the whole thing like a hypeman who never lost a step. Think late-period Beastie Boys energy filtered through a fresh, more electronic lens.

The song actually dropped while Mike D was still on stage at the sold-out Plaza Nightclub and Dance Hall in Los Angeles on the night of May 7, which is about as cool a debut moment as you can engineer. The show drew around 150 people to the intimate cash-only venue, where Mike D also played Beastie Boys classics including "So What'cha Want" and "Looking Down the Barrel of a Gun." The road to this moment began a few weeks earlier when his sons brought him up on stage at surprise California shows, and the crowd response made clear there was a real appetite for new Mike D music.

As for what comes next, Rolling Stone reported that Mike D performed mostly new songs from what sounds like an upcoming album at the Plaza show. No formal album announcement has been made yet, but the live show and the single together paint a picture of an artist with a lot more to say. He has a short run of intimate dates lined up: South Pasadena's Sid the Cat Auditorium on May 10, and two nights at Brooklyn's Xanadu Roller Arts on May 22 and 23. If "Switch Up" is any indication, the wait for whatever comes next will be very much worth it.
keyPoints: Mike D released "Switch Up" on May 8, 2026, his first-ever solo single
It is the first new music from any Beastie Boys member since Hot Sauce Committee Part Two in 2011
The track was created with his sons' band Very Nice Person and co-produced by Carter Lang
"Switch Up" premiered live at a sold-out show at LA's Plaza Nightclub on May 7 while Mike D was still on stage
Mike D has upcoming shows in South Pasadena and Brooklyn, and Rolling Stone reports he performed new songs suggesting a larger project is coming

articles.title: Marathon's Flop Forces Sony to Book $560M Loss on Bungie
articles.summary: Sony has recorded a $560 million impairment charge against Bungie in Q4 of fiscal year 2025, bringing total losses on the $3.6 billion acquisition to roughly $765 million for the year. The write-down comes as Marathon struggles to hold players and Destiny 2's audience continues to shrink. Sony CFO Lin Tao confirmed the studio's games 'did not reach expectations,' but Sony says it is not giving up on Marathon just yet.
articles.content:
Back in 2022, Sony paid $3.6 billion to acquire Bungie, the studio behind Halo and Destiny, betting the deal would supercharge PlayStation's push into live-service multiplayer gaming. Four years later, that bet is looking very costly.

Sony's latest earnings report, dropped on May 8, 2026, revealed a $560 million impairment charge against Bungie in the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025 alone. Sony recorded a total of nearly $765 million lost for the entire year on the Bungie deal, with the possibility of additional losses from the acquisition in fiscal year 2026. Sony CFO Lin Tao was blunt about it on the earnings call. "In our Studio Business, earnings from Bungie's title portfolio did not reach our expectations, so we downwardly revised our business plan and impaired the full amount of the fixed assets related to Bungie except for goodwill," said Tao.

The two culprits are clear. A big portion of that no doubt comes from Destiny 2's player base falling off a cliff after last year's expansion was lackluster and the newest content update was delayed by three months. Then there is Marathon, Bungie's first new franchise in over a decade, which launched on March 5. Two months later, Sony still hasn't confirmed sales numbers for the extraction shooter, let alone total player counts. At the time of writing, Marathon boasted just 6,000 players on Steam, with a 24-hour peak of around 15,000. That is a brutal drop from its all-time peak of over 88,000 concurrent players on Steam at launch.

The big question now is whether Sony cuts its losses or doubles down. For the moment, it is choosing to fight. Sony still plans to continue supporting Marathon thanks to its strong critical reception, pointing to a Metacritic score of 82 and more than 90% of the player reviews on Steam being positive. Still, the human cost inside Bungie is real. Bungie has already seen multiple rounds of mass layoffs since the acquisition, its CEO Pete Parsons was pushed out, and insiders have reportedly flagged the possibility of further cuts if Marathon's player numbers deteriorate further. Sony's overall gaming business is not falling apart. The broader PlayStation business actually held up fine, with gaming operating income rising 12% for the year, and the PS5 has now shipped 93.7 million units. But Bungie stands out as the one sore spot on an otherwise solid report, and that spotlight is not going away anytime soon.
keyPoints: Sony booked a $560 million impairment charge against Bungie in Q4 FY2025, totalling ~$765 million for the full year
Both Marathon and Destiny 2 underperformed, with Marathon averaging just 6,000 concurrent Steam players two months after launch
Sony CFO Lin Tao confirmed Bungie's games 'did not reach expectations' on the earnings call
Sony says it will keep supporting Marathon, citing an 82 Metacritic score and 90%+ positive Steam reviews
Bungie has faced multiple rounds of layoffs since Sony's 2022 acquisition and lost its CEO Pete Parsons

articles.title: Phoebe Bridgers Plays First Solo Show in 3 Years, Debuts New Songs
articles.summary: Phoebe Bridgers returned to the stage on Friday night for her first solo show in three years, playing a surprise 400-capacity gig at The Liberty in Roswell, New Mexico, and debuting new material that strongly hints at a third album. Every fan in attendance received an exclusive card that, when combined with others, appears to form the artwork for her next release. The show was alien-themed, phone-free, and exactly the kind of mysterious, understated comeback fans have come to expect from Bridgers.
articles.content:
Phoebe Bridgers is back. After nearly three years away from solo performing, Bridgers made her long-awaited return on Friday night (May 8) at the 400-capacity venue The Liberty in Roswell, New Mexico. The show was announced with almost no warning, emerging with a subtlety that felt very on-brand. Fans first spotted the gig through a printed flyer circulating online before any official confirmation appeared on her website or the venue's socials.

For context, her last time performing solo was at New Jersey's MetLife Stadium in the spring of 2023, when she was an opening act on Taylor Swift's Eras Tour. In between, she continued playing shows with Boygenius, her supergroup with equally brilliant songwriters Lucy Dacus and Julien Baker, and they made an appearance on Saturday Night Live in November 2023 and played a pair of acoustic shows the following February at the Smell in Los Angeles, where they announced their hiatus. Since then, Bridgers has largely stayed out of the public eye.

The Roswell show itself was exactly the kind of event to send the internet into a spin. The performance took place in front of an alien-themed stage with neon-painted figures, and Bridgers was joined by Christian Lee Hutson. She explained that the show was a "test" for upcoming live performances. No cameras, phones, smart glasses, or other recording devices were allowed inside. That made the scraps of fan reports that trickled out feel all the more precious.

Bridgers debuted three new songs during the show, suggesting that a new album may be on its way. Fans in attendance each received an exclusive card, which it appears can be combined to make up the artwork for Bridgers' next release. Fans report receiving a letter in their Yondr pouches containing a random part of what is possibly a Chris Riddell illustration, and they are speculating that together they form the artwork for Bridgers' next album cover. Riddell was behind the booklet for her last record, Punisher.

Bridgers played several songs from her two solo albums, Stranger In The Alps and Punisher, while the new songs have been described by those in attendance as "very sad folk" with harmonica arrangements. Fans also report that one of the new songs was about the death of her father, who passed away in 2023. And if the alien theme, the Roswell location, and the puzzle-piece artwork were not enough hints, fans on social media are already speculating that Bridgers' third album will be alien-themed. A new Phoebe Bridgers album era, if it is coming, could not have a more fitting launch pad.
keyPoints: Phoebe Bridgers played her first solo show in three years at The Liberty in Roswell, New Mexico on May 8
She debuted new songs described as "very sad folk" with harmonica, hinting at a third album
Every fan got an exclusive card that appears to piece together into the new album's artwork
The show was alien-themed and entirely phone-free, announced via a printed flyer with almost no notice
Bridgers also played songs from both her solo albums, Stranger In The Alps and Punisher, and was joined on stage by Christian Lee Hutson

articles.title: Trump Drops 162 UFO Files. Here's What's Actually In Them.
articles.summary: The Trump administration released 162 declassified UFO files on May 8, 2026, including videos, photos, and documents spanning nearly 80 years of government sightings. The files were posted publicly on a new Department of War website with no security clearance required. The release is the biggest government UFO disclosure in years, but experts say nothing in it definitively proves alien life exists.
articles.content:
On Friday, May 8, Donald Trump made good on one of his more unusual promises. The Department of War dropped 162 declassified files on unidentified anomalous phenomena, or UAPs, the government's preferred modern term for UFOs. The files went live on a newly launched website under the Pentagon's PURSUE program, which stands for Presidential Unsealing and Reporting System for UAP Encounters. Anyone can read them. No clearance required.

The batch includes 120 PDFs, 28 videos, and 14 images gathered from agencies including the Department of Defense, the FBI, NASA, and the State Department. The records cover incidents stretching from 1948 all the way to 2026. One of the more striking documents is a transcript from the 1965 Gemini 7 space mission, in which astronaut Frank Borman reported spotting a "bogey" along with hundreds of tiny particles in space. A handwritten note on the file reads "UFO Sighting by Borman." There are also reports of unexplained objects seen over Kuwait, Iraq, Syria, Greece, Japan, Kazakhstan, and Africa.

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said in a statement that the files had been "hidden behind classifications" for too long, adding "it's time the American people see it for themselves." Trump posted a characteristically colorful statement calling the release an act of "Complete and Maximum Transparency" and inviting the public to decide for themselves "WHAT THE HELL IS GOING ON." NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman also praised the move on X, saying the agency would stay "candid about what we know to be true."

Here is the catch though. Analysts say the files do not deliver a smoking gun. Experts pointed out that many of the documents had already been partially released before, and some of the new videos show little more than unidentified white dots at a distance. The Pentagon itself included a disclaimer warning that much of the material has not been analyzed for resolution of any anomalies. Government officials confirmed the files contain no verified evidence of extraterrestrial life or technology. Independent journalist Leslie Kean, who co-authored the 2017 New York Times story that blew open the Pentagon's secret UAP program, put it plainly: "I think we've already proven the existence of UAP, but that doesn't mean we've proven they're alien." The Pentagon promised more tranches of files will follow on a rolling basis, so the disclosure is far from over.
keyPoints: The Trump administration released 162 UFO files on May 8, 2026, under the PURSUE program
The files include 28 videos, 14 images, and 120 PDFs from agencies like the FBI, NASA, and the Pentagon
Highlights include a 1965 Gemini 7 astronaut transcript reporting a UFO sighting
No verified evidence of extraterrestrial life or technology was found in the files
More batches are expected to be released on a rolling basis in the coming weeks
articles.wikiSearchQuery: UAP UFO government disclosure United States 2026

articles.title: Katy Perry, Future, Lisa and Tyla to Open FIFA World Cup 2026
articles.summary: FIFA has confirmed a blockbuster lineup for the 2026 World Cup opening ceremonies across three countries, with Katy Perry, Future, Lisa, Tyla, Anitta and Rema headlining the US show on June 12. It marks the first time FIFA has staged multiple opening ceremonies across different host nations for a single tournament. The US ceremony takes place at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California, before the United States faces Paraguay.
articles.content:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is not wasting any time making history. Opening celebrations will be held in Mexico, the US, and Toronto — marking the first time FIFA has featured multiple opening ceremonies across different countries for a single tournament. That alone is a big deal. But the lineups they have put together make it even bigger.

Katy Perry, Future, Anitta, Lisa, Rema, and Tyla will headline the celebrations on June 12 at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California. The US will face Paraguay in the tournament's opening match. Katy Perry announced the news on her Instagram with a playful nod to her own catalog, writing: "Kicking my feet because I'm performing at the @fifaworldcup 2026 Opening Ceremony in Los Angeles on June 12th! Stay tuned for more info — don't let this be #TheOneThatGotAway."

The opening ceremony in Mexico will include J Balvin, Tyla, Lila Downs, Los Ángeles Azules, Maná, Alejandro Fernández, Belinda, and Danny Ocean before the tournament opener on June 11 between Mexico and South Africa at Mexico City's Azteca Stadium. In Canada, Alanis Morissette, Alessia Cara, Elyanna, Jessie Reyez, Michael Bublé, Nora Fatei, Sanjoy, Vegedream and William Prince will take the stage to celebrate the opener against Bosnia and Herzegovina at Toronto's BMO Field.

Lisa is officially set to headline the USA opening ceremony, and with the performance the BLACKPINK star will become only the second K-pop idol after BTS's Jungkook to perform at a FIFA World Cup opening ceremony. This year's World Cup takes place between June 11 and July 19, 2026 across 16 cities in Canada, Mexico and the United States, marking the first time the tournament has been co-hosted across multiple countries. FIFA has yet to announce talent slated to grace the stage for the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final Halftime Show on July 19 at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. More names are still coming, so stay tuned.
keyPoints: Katy Perry, Future, Lisa, Anitta, Rema and Tyla headline the US opening ceremony at SoFi Stadium on June 12
It is the first time FIFA has held opening ceremonies in multiple countries for one tournament
Mexico's ceremony on June 11 at Azteca Stadium features J Balvin, Maná, Tyla, Belinda and more
Canada's Toronto ceremony includes Alanis Morissette, Michael Bublé, Alessia Cara and Jessie Reyez
Lisa will become only the second K-pop artist ever to perform at a World Cup opening ceremony

articles.title: Tesla's Cybertruck Gets Its 11th Recall: Wheels Can Fall Off
articles.summary: Tesla has issued its 11th recall for the Cybertruck, this time because wheels on certain rear-wheel-drive models can literally detach while driving. The culprit is cracked brake rotors caused by wrong grease on the lug nuts, which can cause the wheel studs to separate from the hub entirely. The kicker: the recall only covers 173 vehicles, quietly revealing just how few people actually bought the discontinued $70,000 Cybertruck RWD model.
articles.content:
Tesla's Cybertruck is back in the headlines for all the wrong reasons. The company has issued its 11th recall for the angular stainless steel truck, and this one is hard to spin: the wheels can fall off while you are driving.

The recall targets the rear-wheel-drive Cybertruck, a cheaper $70,000 variant Tesla launched about a year ago as a more accessible entry point into the Cybertruck lineup. According to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, cracks can form in the brake rotor stud holes due to road stress. Once those cracks spread, the wheel studs can separate from the hub, making the vehicle hard to control and potentially sending a wheel flying off entirely. The root cause, per Wired, was wrong grease applied to the lug nuts during production, combined with loose nuts that could not hold their torque under load.

Here is where things get awkward. The recall covers every single rear-wheel-drive Cybertruck Tesla ever sold: a grand total of 173 vehicles. That number, now public thanks to the recall filing, confirms what many suspected: the Cybertruck RWD was a quiet flop. Tesla had already discontinued the model in November 2025, just months after launching it. Tesla says it has identified three warranty claims potentially linked to the issue, and as of now there are no reported collisions, injuries, or fatalities. Owner notifications began on April 24, and Tesla will replace the brake rotors, hubs, and lug nuts free of charge, with full remedy communications going out to all affected owners by June 20, 2026.

This is far from the Cybertruck's first rodeo with recalls. Previous issues have included an accelerator pedal that could get stuck in the down position, stainless steel body panels and the lightbar attachment flying off due to bad adhesive, and cameras losing function. For a vehicle Elon Musk once called the "finest in apocalypse technology," the Cybertruck has spent much of its life in the shop rather than surviving the apocalypse.
keyPoints: Tesla issued its 11th Cybertruck recall over wheels that can detach while driving
The problem is cracked brake rotors caused by wrong grease on lug nuts, affecting 2024–2026 rear-wheel-drive models with 18-inch steel wheels
Only 173 Cybertruck RWD vehicles were ever sold, a number now confirmed by the recall filing
Tesla discontinued the $70,000 RWD model in November 2025 due to poor sales
Tesla will replace brake rotors, hubs, and lug nuts for free, with owner letters going out by June 20, 2026

articles.title: Red Hot Chili Peppers Sells Recorded Catalog to WMG for $300M+
articles.summary: Red Hot Chili Peppers has sold its recorded music catalog to Warner Music Group for more than $300 million, confirmed by multiple sources on May 8, 2026. Warner Music Group made the purchase through its $1.2 billion joint venture with Bain Capital, with the Red Hot Chili Peppers deal alone accounting for roughly half of the $650 million the venture has spent so far. The catalog generates an estimated $26 million in annual revenue and includes landmark albums like Blood Sugar Sex Magik and Californication.
articles.content:
Red Hot Chili Peppers has officially sold its recorded music catalog to Warner Music Group for more than $300 million. The deal was first reported by The Hollywood Reporter on May 8, 2026, and sources confirmed the details to Billboard shortly after. It is one of the biggest catalog sales in rock music history.

Warner Music Group first announced its $1.2 billion joint venture catalog acquisition vehicle with Bain Capital last July and reported in its May 7 earnings report that it had spent $650 million on catalogs since the joint venture's launch, though the company did not specify what those acquisitions were. The Red Hot Chili Peppers deal makes up about half of that $650 million figure.

According to Billboard estimates published last year, the Red Hot Chili Peppers master recording catalog generates about $26 million in revenue annually, with the majority of that coming from Warner Music Group's part of the catalog, including smash albums like Blood Sugar Sex Magik and Californication. Warner is a natural choice for the band to sell to given that Red Hot Chili Peppers has been signed to flagship label Warner Records since the early 1990s, where they have released every one of their albums since 1991's Blood Sugar Sex Magik.

This is not the first time the band has cashed in on its legacy. News of the sale to Warner Music Group comes five years after the band sold its music publishing catalog to Hipgnosis for between $140 million and $150 million. The publishing rights could be changing hands again soon, as Sony Music Group is currently in talks to acquire Recognition — the company formerly known as Hipgnosis — in what would be a multibillion-dollar deal.

The catalog acquisition space remains hot as the major labels have all partnered with outside investors to buy up music rights. Aside from Warner, Universal Music Group has a minority stake in Chord Music Partners, while Sony recently announced a venture with Singapore's GIC. For Red Hot Chili Peppers, the deal locks in generational wealth from decades of hits while the band's music keeps streaming and syncing its way to tens of millions of dollars every year.
keyPoints: Red Hot Chili Peppers sold its recorded catalog to Warner Music Group for over $300 million
Warner Music Group made the purchase through its $1.2 billion joint venture with Bain Capital
The catalog brings in roughly $26 million in annual revenue and includes Blood Sugar Sex Magik and Californication
The band previously sold its publishing catalog to Hipgnosis for $140–$150 million back in 2021
Major labels are increasingly partnering with private equity to buy up iconic music rights

articles.title: Mario Throws Lore-Accurate First Pitch at Yokohama Baystars Game
articles.summary: Nintendo's Mario threw a ceremonial first pitch at the Yokohama Baystars' home game on May 5, 2026, and fans immediately noticed the throw was completely true to Super Mario Bros. game physics. The pitch bounced along the ground just like Mario's in-game fireballs do, making it one of the most delightfully on-brand sports moments in recent memory. The appearance is part of Nintendo's massive Mario 40th anniversary sponsorship across all 12 Nippon Professional Baseball teams.
articles.content:
Nintendo is celebrating Mario's 40th anniversary in a very unexpected place: the baseball diamond. As part of a sponsorship deal covering all 12 teams in Japan's Nippon Professional Baseball league, Mario is throwing the ceremonial first pitch at home games running from April 29 through June 21, 2026. The Yokohama Baystars hosted their turn on May 5, and the internet immediately lost its mind.

When Mario stepped up to the mound at Yokohama Stadium to face the Hiroshima Carp, he did not exactly fire a blazing fastball down the middle. Instead, his pitch bounced along the ground in a short, low arc. And fans were quick to point out that this was not a bad throw. It was a perfect one. In Super Mario Bros., when Mario throws a fireball, it travels a short distance and then bounces. Mario's ceremonial pitch followed that exact same trajectory, which is about as lore-accurate as it gets.

The fun did not stop at the pitcher's mound. The bases at Yokohama Stadium were swapped out for Super Mario Question Block designs, and fans got the chance to run the bases themselves after the game through a lottery. Luigi and Princess Peach also made appearances at the nearby Basegate entertainment complex for a fan meet-and-greet, while the Baystars' cheerleading squad called Diana wore special Toad-themed caps in honor of the collaboration.

Unfortunately for the Baystars, Mario's good vibes did not translate to a home win. The Hiroshima Carp absolutely dominated, winning 10-0. But honestly, the scoreboard barely matters here. A video game character throwing a perfectly in-universe baseball pitch at a professional sporting event is the kind of crossover moment that reminds you why Nintendo has been winning hearts for four decades.
keyPoints: Mario threw a bouncing first pitch at the Yokohama Baystars' May 5 game, matching the fireball physics from Super Mario Bros.
Nintendo is sponsoring home games for all 12 Nippon Professional Baseball teams from April 29 to June 21 as part of Mario's 40th anniversary.
Question Block bases replaced normal bases at Yokohama Stadium, and fans could run the bases after the game via lottery.
Luigi and Princess Peach also appeared at a nearby fan meet-and-greet, and the Baystars' cheer squad wore Toad hats.
The Baystars lost the game 10-0 to the Hiroshima Carp, but the internet didn't really care.

articles.title: NewJeans and HYBE Sued Over 'How Sweet' for Blatant Copying
articles.summary: Four songwriters — Audrey Armacost, Aidan Rodriguez, Adam Gokcebay, and Michael Campanelli — have filed a U.S. copyright lawsuit against NewJeans members, their agency ADOR, and parent company HYBE, claiming the 2024 hit 'How Sweet' copied their rejected demo 'One of a Kind.' The writers say they submitted the demo for NewJeans to consider, were told it wasn't selected, and then heard their own melody show up in 'How Sweet' four months later. The song hit No. 7 on the Billboard Global Excl. U.S. chart, and the writers want a cut of those royalties.
articles.content:
This is the kind of story that makes the music business feel like a minefield. Four songwriters — Audrey Armacost, Aidan Rodriguez, Adam Gokcebay, and Michael Campanelli — have sued the NewJeans members, their agency ADOR, and its parent company HYBE over purported similarities between NewJeans' 2024 hit 'How Sweet' and a demo submitted during the songwriting process.

Here is how it allegedly went down. Audrey Armacost "was sent an instrumental track by her publisher in January 2024 and was invited to submit topline lyrics and melody for consideration by NewJeans." The four songwriters claim that they sent a demo written and recorded over that instrumental, titled 'One of a Kind,' to NewJeans, but were told that it had not been selected. Yet when 'How Sweet' was released four months later, the lawsuit claims the song's first verse was "quantitatively and qualitatively similar" to the first verse in 'One of a Kind.'

The complaint gets very specific about what was allegedly copied. The songwriters claim the first verse of 'How Sweet' is "quantitatively and qualitatively similar" to the first verse of their demo, adding: "Both works are in 4/4 meter and the key of B flat minor. And both works contain a topline that includes an approximately eight-bar, 31-note melodic sequence consisting of four series." Their lawyer Trevor Barrett put it plainly: "Adam, Aidan, Audrey and Michael are accomplished songwriters who were deeply disappointed to discover this blatant copying of their original song, 'One of a Kind,' in NewJeans' 'How Sweet.' They look forward to having their rights acknowledged and vindicated via this lawsuit."

Through the lawsuit, the four songwriters are seeking a cut of the royalties from the "great commercial success" of 'How Sweet.' ADOR fired back quickly. On May 9 KST, a representative of ADOR told media outlets, "We have confirmed with the production team behind 'How Sweet' at BANA that there was no plagiarism involved. Based on this, we plan to respond to the lawsuit accordingly."

This lawsuit lands at a particularly rough time for NewJeans and HYBE. The new lawsuit comes in the midst of an ongoing reconciliation between some members of NewJeans and ADOR following a protracted legal battle of their own. In 2024, all five original NewJeans members attempted to sever ties with HYBE and go independent under the new moniker NJZ following the controversial firing of their mentor, former ADOR CEO Min Hee-jin. This led to a lawsuit, and ADOR ultimately prevailed when a South Korean court ruled in October that the women must remain under an exclusive contract until 2029. Since then, Haerin, Hyein, and Hanni have returned to ADOR, while Danielle's exclusive contract was terminated. Minji is still "discussing her future activities" with the company, ADOR said as recently as May 7 KST. The group can barely catch a breath.
keyPoints: Four songwriters claim NewJeans' 'How Sweet' copied their rejected demo 'One of a Kind'
The demo was submitted in January 2024 and rejected, then 'How Sweet' dropped four months later
Plaintiffs cite matching key, time signature, and a near-identical 31-note melodic sequence
ADOR denies the allegations, citing confirmation from producers BANA that no plagiarism occurred
The lawsuit hits as NewJeans are still navigating a separate contract dispute that has split the group

articles.title: adidas A-TYPE Goes Public: $1,000 Sneakers, $3,000 Tracksuits
articles.summary: adidas has opened its ultra-luxury A-TYPE line to the public for the first time, with the SS26 collection dropping May 7 through just eight select boutiques worldwide. The all-black range includes the A-LEATHER Superstar at $1,000 and the Archetype Firebird tracksuit pieces at $1,500 each, adding up to over $4,000 for the full look. Until now, A-TYPE was strictly friends and family only — making this the first time regular shoppers can actually buy in.
articles.content:
adidas has been quietly building something wild. The A-TYPE line launched last year as an invite-only, artisanal range of Italian-made sportswear — the kind of thing you only got your hands on if you were already in the inner circle. Launched as an "artisanal" line of three-striped sportswear made in Italy, its early pieces included $800 sneakers with cashmere laces and handmade leather handbags, strictly for friends and family only. Now, for the first time, adidas has opened A-TYPE to everyone. Well, everyone with a very deep wallet.

The adidas A-TYPE SS26 collection released May 7 exclusively through eight select retail partners, including A Ma Maniere in Atlanta and ESSX in New York. Those wanting to purchase A-TYPE can't do so on adidas' website — it's only available at top-tier boutiques like Slam Jam in Italy, ESSX in New York, and The Broken Arm in Paris. So yes, it's public now, but it still requires some effort to find.

Unveiled in a photoshoot with French footballer Ousmane Dembélé, A-TYPE's second collection is less colorful and more low-key than its first round of quiet luxury sportswear. Everything is all black. The shell-toed adidas Superstar is once again A-TYPE's sneaker of choice, created from calf leather and real silver hardware. This time the shoe is entirely black, as is the rest of the collection. The refined pair is finished with cashmere shoelaces with detachable lace tips, silver-plated lace jewelry, and a Lightstrike Pro midsole. The Superstars arrive in a molded shoe box accompanied by wooden shoe trees, reinforcing the collection's luxury positioning. That box alone feels like an unboxing event.

Pair those $1,000 sneakers with a black leather Firebird tracksuit — loose leather pants and a boxy track jacket, both costing $1,500 apiece. Yes, that's over $4,000 for the full look. Ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, adidas also created football-inspired A-TYPE pieces including a jersey, football, and carrier — but those items will remain Friends and Family exclusives and will not release to the public. So even within the new "public" drop, there's still a layer of exclusivity above it.

adidas has quietly been ramping up its production of both German-made and Italian-made sneakers, drip-feeding more elevated products with elevated prices into its range. These have flown largely under the radar, partly because of a low-key marketing campaign. But a $1,000 Superstar with cashmere laces is impossible to ignore for long. The brand that built its name on accessible streetwear is now testing just how far its loyal fans will follow it upmarket. If A-TYPE keeps selling out, don't be surprised when the Stan Smith gets the same treatment next.
keyPoints: adidas A-TYPE SS26 dropped May 7, 2026 — the first time the line is available to the general public
The A-LEATHER Superstar costs $1,000 and is made in Italy from calf leather with silver hardware and cashmere laces
The Archetype Firebird leather tracksuit top and pants are $1,500 each, making the full look over $4,000
It's sold only at eight select boutiques worldwide, not on adidas' own website
Football-inspired pieces including a jersey and collectible ball remain friends and family exclusives only

articles.title: Tony Kanal Cried at Soundcheck Before No Doubt's Sphere Debut
articles.summary: No Doubt bassist Tony Kanal opened up about the raw emotion of the band's first show at Sphere in Las Vegas on May 6, 2026, telling Variety he had "a good cry at soundcheck" before taking the stage. The 18-date residency marks No Doubt's first extended run of shows in nearly 14 years and celebrates the 30th anniversary of Tragic Kingdom. No Doubt also made history as the first female-fronted act to headline Sphere.
articles.content:
No Doubt's long-awaited return to the stage landed in the biggest possible way. It was mere hours before No Doubt kicked off its residency at Las Vegas' Sphere when the emotional weight of the moment struck bassist Tony Kanal. "I had a good cry at soundcheck," he told Variety of the group's Wednesday night performance, the first of 18 shows at the state-of-the-art venue. The tears made total sense once you understand how long this moment took to build.

The performance was the culmination of discussions that firmed up in September 2025, when the band agreed to a residency and set off on a sprint to assemble a four-act spectacle that captured the grit and grind of the group's ascent. "Every waking moment over the past eight months was about this show," Kanal said. No Doubt, comprised of Gwen Stefani, Tony Kanal, Tom Dumont, and Adrian Young, announced their long-awaited return with a limited engagement in May and June 2026 at Sphere at The Venetian. The shows mark the band's first extended run in nearly 14 years.

The opening night setlist was packed with surprises. The night's biggest shock arrived immediately. Instead of easing in with a familiar hit, No Doubt opened with "Tragic Kingdom," the sprawling title track from their 1995 breakthrough album. The song hadn't been played live in 17 years, and the audience erupted the second those opening notes rang out. The band also delivered fan favorites including "Don't Speak," "Hella Good," "Ex-Girlfriend," and "Just a Girl," backed by immersive visuals and rare archival footage from the group's early days. Deep cuts like "Trapped in a Box" and "The Climb" — played live for the first time since 1997 — were also peppered into the set.

No Doubt also became the first female-led act to headline Sphere. That milestone adds another layer to an already historic run. The residency marks the 30th anniversary of No Doubt's seminal album Tragic Kingdom, which made the band a household name and one of the most celebrated acts of their generation.

For No Doubt, the Sphere residency comes in the wake of a long period of relative dormancy. The Southern California band last put out an album in 2012 with Push and Shove, and performances have been generally sporadic since, save for some festival dates over the years. Kanal summed up what playing together again feels like: "There is a beautiful energy that happens when we play together, an electricity I have felt through all of our years." With 18 shows running through mid-June, No Doubt is giving fans who waited more than a decade plenty of chances to feel it too.
keyPoints: No Doubt kicked off an 18-date Sphere residency in Las Vegas on May 6, 2026
Tony Kanal admitted he cried at soundcheck before the emotional first show
The band opened with "Tragic Kingdom," a song not played live since 2009
No Doubt made history as the first female-fronted act to headline Sphere
The residency celebrates the 30th anniversary of Tragic Kingdom and is the band's first extended run in nearly 14 years

articles.title: Apple's iOS 18.1 Lets Others Record Your iPhone Calls Without Your Control
articles.summary: Since iOS 18.1, Apple has quietly made it easy for anyone on an iPhone call to record every word you say, and you have no way to stop them short of hanging up. The feature does play an automatic Siri notification when recording starts, but the person being recorded gets zero control over it. That gap between Apple's privacy reputation and how this feature actually works is raising real questions.
articles.content:
Here is something worth knowing before your next iPhone call. The next time you take a call on your iPhone, the other person could be recording every word you say, and you may not even realize it. Ever since iOS 18.1, Apple has offered a call recording feature on its iPhones. It sounds useful on paper, and it is, but there is a side of it that Apple has not been loud about.

When iOS 18.1 shipped in the fall of 2024 with the Phone app's call recording feature enabled, it did not offer the person being recorded any control over the recording, nor did it provide a reasonable notification that the recording was in progress. This has not changed to this day, even with the recent release of iOS 26.4. So if someone on the other end of your call taps the record button, you are along for the ride.

Here is how it works in practice. While on a voice call, any party using an iPhone on iOS 18.1 can tap the "Call Recording" button in the top-left corner of their screen. A countdown begins on their phone, and once it finishes, Siri automatically alerts all participants that the call is being recorded. So there is a Siri voice alert. But here is the catch. Only the party that initiated the recording can stop it by tapping the "Call Recording" button again. If a party on the call does not want to have the call recorded, they either need to hang up or request that the recording party stop it. There is no other way to stop or opt out of the recording. The recording will continue until it is either stopped or the call is terminated.

There is also a practical loophole worth knowing. There is an option to disable call recording on your own iPhone via Settings, then Apps, then Phone, then Call Recording. Toggling the setting off simply removes the call recording button from your own Phone interface. It will not prevent anyone else from starting a recording during your phone calls. On top of that, after the call ends, a Notes app notification guides the party that initiated the recording to a newly created note containing the call recording and its transcription. The whole thing is seamless for the recorder. For everyone else, it is a little unsettling. The iPhone feature seems antithetical to Apple's privacy stance.
keyPoints: iOS 18.1 added native call recording to the iPhone Phone app back in fall 2024
Siri announces the recording to all parties, but only the person who started it can stop it
The person being recorded cannot block or opt out of a recording mid-call
Turning off call recording in your own iPhone settings only removes the button for you — not for the other person
After a call, the full recording and transcript are saved automatically to the recorder's Notes app

articles.title: Rolling Stones Cover Amy Winehouse on New Album Foreign Tongues
articles.summary: The Rolling Stones have revealed that their upcoming album Foreign Tongues includes a cover of Amy Winehouse's 'You Know I'm No Good,' from her 2006 classic Back to Black. The news dropped live on The Tonight Show when Jimmy Fallon read the tracklist off a vinyl copy while Ronnie Wood was a guest. Foreign Tongues drops July 10 and is already stacked with star power, featuring Paul McCartney, Robert Smith, Steve Winwood, and even the late Charlie Watts.
articles.content:
The Rolling Stones and Amy Winehouse is a combination nobody saw coming — but it makes perfect sense. Foreign Tongues, the band's new album, will feature a cover of Winehouse's 'You Know I'm No Good,' the cheeky cut from her classic 2006 album Back to Black. And the way the world found out was brilliantly spontaneous.

During a Tonight Show interview on May 7, guitarist Ronnie Wood watched as Jimmy Fallon recited the vinyl tracklist for Foreign Tongues, due out July 10. "You do a cover of Amy Winehouse!" Fallon exclaimed, to raucous audience applause. The pair then broke into an a capella version of the song, clapping and dancing along as they conjured the jazzy rhythm. It was one of those genuinely joyful TV moments.

The tribute carries real weight. The song originally released on Back to Black turns twenty years old this autumn. The Stones and Winehouse are no strangers either — in a famous Isle of Wight Festival performance in 2007, they teamed up to sing a rollicking version of the Temptations' 'Ain't Too Proud to Beg.' Covering her work on Foreign Tongues feels like a full-circle moment for both legacies.

Foreign Tongues is shaping up to be one of the most exciting Stones records in years. The album will feature guest appearances from Paul McCartney, Robert Smith, Steve Winwood, and the band's late drummer Charlie Watts. Robert Smith's appearance was a particular surprise — The Cure frontman was in the same London studio as the Stones when Mick Jagger ran into him. Jagger recalled the moment: "I said, 'I've never met you before, but you're Robert Smith of the Cure.' He said, 'Yeah!' I said, 'While you're here, we better go and do something.' He sings a backing vocal."

The album is made up of 14 tracks, including lead single 'In The Stars' and the raw, bluesy 'Rough And Twisted,' and it will be released on July 10 via Polydor/Universal Music. Foreign Tongues follows up 2023's celebrated Hackney Diamonds, which was a Number Three hit in the U.S. and won the band a Grammy for Best Rock Album. If Foreign Tongues even gets close to that, this summer is going to be a very good one.
keyPoints: Rolling Stones will cover Amy Winehouse's 'You Know I'm No Good' on Foreign Tongues
The news was revealed live on The Tonight Show by Jimmy Fallon reading the vinyl tracklist
Winehouse and the Stones performed together at the 2007 Isle of Wight Festival
Foreign Tongues also features Paul McCartney, Robert Smith, Steve Winwood, and Charlie Watts
The album drops July 10 via Polydor/Universal Music

articles.title: Sydney Sweeney's Euphoria Season 3 Cocaine Scene Divides Fans
articles.summary: Sydney Sweeney has shocked Euphoria viewers again in Season 3 Episode 4, this time with a scene involving cocaine rather than nudity. The moment immediately went viral on X and Reddit, splitting fans between outrage and praise for what many called the season's most entertaining episode yet. Euphoria Season 3 continues to stream on HBO Max with new episodes every Sunday until the finale on May 31, 2026.
articles.content:
Euphoria Season 3 has shaken fans with a scene so controversial that it instantly started a full-blown debate across social media platforms like X and Reddit. And yes, Sydney Sweeney's character Cassie is once again at the centre of it all.

A scene in the episode saw Cassie snorting cocaine off a friend's crotch, before rubbing the same drug on herself. That is the scene. That is what happened. Viewers were not exactly ready for it, with one fan writing on X: "Cassie rubbed cocaine on her WHAT?!?! #euphoria."

To understand how Cassie got here, a little backstory helps. Season 3 picks up with Rue (Zendaya) and her friends after a five-year time jump, and Cassie turns to creating adult content to earn extra cash for her wedding. After last week's devastating fallout from her wedding to Nate, Cassie doubles down on her money-making schemes. The cocaine scene is the latest escalation in a storyline that has been dividing audiences since episode one.

Euphoria's third and most likely final season has been panned by critics, but it is Sydney's scenes that have faced the most scrutiny, with some branding them "degrading", "horrible" and comparable to a "humiliation ritual." Euphoria creator Sam Levinson has pushed back, praising Sweeney's commitment on set. Levinson has said she "becomes brilliant" when he "push[es] it a little bit" during the show's high-pressure filming process.

On X, many fans called Episode 4 the season's best, largely because it reunites the core cast: Zendaya, Sydney Sweeney, Alexa Demie, and Maude Apatow. Season 3 will continue releasing new episodes every Sunday, with the finale set for May 31, 2026. Love it or hate it, Euphoria still knows how to keep people talking.
keyPoints: Euphoria Season 3 Episode 4 features a cocaine scene involving Cassie (Sydney Sweeney) that went instantly viral
The scene is separate from the show's ongoing OnlyFans storyline for Cassie's character
Creator Sam Levinson has defended the scenes, praising Sweeney's on-set performance
Critics have largely panned Season 3, calling some of Sweeney's scenes 'degrading'
New episodes drop every Sunday with the Season 3 finale set for May 31, 2026 on HBO Max

articles.title: How 'Hatha-Hate' Cost Anne Hathaway Millions in Film Roles
articles.summary: A wave of intense online hate in the 2010s, dubbed 'Hatha-hate', cost Anne Hathaway millions of dollars in lost film roles and seriously damaged her mental health. The backlash was so widespread and toxic that it became a named cultural phenomenon, making Hathaway one of the earliest high-profile victims of celebrity cancel culture. She has since spoken candidly about how humiliating and destabilising the experience was.
articles.content:
Long before 'cancel culture' became a household phrase, Anne Hathaway lived through one of its most brutal early chapters. In the 2010s, a wave of online hate so intense it earned its own name — 'Hatha-hate' — swept across the internet, targeting Hathaway with a level of vitriol that felt wildly out of proportion to anything she had actually done.

The consequences were real and financially devastating. The campaign was so toxic that Hathaway lost film roles as a direct result, with studios reportedly nervous about attaching her name to projects at the height of the backlash. The hate was not just a social media pile-on. It translated into cold, hard career damage and millions in lost earnings at what should have been the peak of her star power.

The roots of 'Hatha-hate' are worth understanding. It exploded around the time Hathaway was winning every award in sight for Les Misérables, including the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress in 2013. Ironically, her visible enthusiasm for those wins, the tearful speeches and the seeming desperation to be loved, became the thing people turned against her. She was accused of being 'try-hard' and inauthentic, which on the early internet was practically a capital offence. The criticism snowballed fast and became genuinely humiliating.

Beyond the career hits, Hathaway has spoken about the toll the campaign took on her mental health. Being the subject of coordinated, gleeful hate is not something anyone walks away from easily, and she has been open about how deeply it affected her sense of self during that period. It is a reminder of just how much damage the internet can do to a real person, particularly a woman who dares to be openly ambitious.

The story has a second act, and it is a good one. Hathaway has spoken about the way public sentiment eventually shifted back in her favour, with a full-blown 'Anne Hathaway Renaissance' taking hold in recent years. Audiences started re-examining why they ever turned on her in the first place, and many found the original 'Hatha-hate' to be embarrassing in hindsight. She is now widely celebrated as one of the most genuinely talented actors of her generation, and the backlash looks, with time, like a case study in how misogyny and mob mentality can briefly rewrite a person's public story.
keyPoints: The 'Hatha-hate' online campaign in the 2010s cost Anne Hathaway millions in lost film roles
The backlash was described as 'toxic and humiliating' and seriously affected her mental health
The hate began around the time she won the Oscar for Best Supporting Actress for Les Misérables
Critics accused her of being try-hard and inauthentic, which fuelled the pile-on
Public sentiment has since shifted with a widely recognised 'Anne Hathaway Renaissance'

articles.title: Amazon Prime Video Adds Vertical 'Clips' Feed Like Netflix and Disney+
articles.summary: Amazon Prime Video is rolling out a vertical short-form video feed called Clips, letting users swipe through short videos from shows and movies before jumping into full titles. The move follows similar features already launched by Netflix and Disney+, signaling a broader shift in how streaming platforms are trying to hook viewers. Users will also be able to rent or buy titles directly from the Clips feed.
articles.content:
Vertical video has officially taken over streaming. Amazon Prime Video is the latest platform to embrace the swipe-up format with a new feature called Clips, a short-form vertical video feed built right into the Prime Video app.

Amazon Prime Video is joining Netflix and Disney+ in adding a vertical feed of videos to its streaming app. The new Clips feed is designed to surface short videos pulled from shows and movies on Prime Video, giving you a quick taste of something before you decide to commit to a full watch.

The feature is not just about browsing. Users will have the option to jump straight into a full title, or rent or buy it, all from inside the Clips feed itself. That makes it less of a discovery toy and more of a direct sales tool dressed up in TikTok clothing.

It is easy to see why Amazon Prime Video is making this move. Shorter attention spans and the dominance of vertical video on platforms like TikTok and Instagram have pushed every major streaming service to rethink how people find new content. Netflix and Disney+ already made similar bets, and now Amazon Prime Video is following suit. The question is whether viewers actually want their streaming app to feel like a social media feed, or whether they would rather just search for something and press play.
keyPoints: Amazon Prime Video is launching a new vertical short-form video feature called Clips
The Clips feed shows short videos from Prime Video shows and movies
Users can jump into full titles or rent and buy content directly from the Clips feed
Netflix and Disney+ already have similar vertical video features
The move reflects how streaming platforms are borrowing from TikTok-style discovery

articles.title: 2013 Scooby-Doo Flash Game Goes Viral for Its Usher Remix
articles.summary: Scooby-Doo Creepy Run, a forgotten 2013 browser game on Kongregate, has exploded across TikTok and Instagram in May 2026 thanks to its chaotic soundtrack, a 2005 Usher remix known as 'Usher Scooby Doo Party Break.' Streamer RatBagTrash called it a 'hidden gem' and his clip racked up 1.8 million TikTok views in a week. The internet is now completely obsessed with a song that has been sitting in the wild since 2005.
articles.content:
Every so often the internet digs up a forgotten relic and loses its mind over it. This week's find is Scooby-Doo Creepy Run, a tiny browser Flash game that most people missed entirely when it quietly launched back in 2013.

Scooby-Doo Creepy Run is a browser-based Flash video game where players take on the role of Shaggy from Scooby-Doo and must run away from a skeleton as fast as possible. The game was published to Kongregate by funnychasegames, also known as 123Chase, on December 3rd, 2013, and is set to a 2005 remix of the Usher, Lil Jon and Ludacris song "Yeah!" known as "Usher Scooby Doo Party Break." The song itself is even older than the game. The remix was posted to YouTube as early as September 12th, 2008, by user DaKleneRobat, gaining over 91,000 views in 18 years. The song seems to predate this upload, with the video claiming it was first made in 2005. So yes, this track has been out there for over 20 years and has only now found its moment.

The spark that set everything off came from streamer RatBagTrash. On April 30th, 2026, a clip of RatBagTrash playing the game was posted to his Instagram and TikTok pages, gaining over 120,000 likes on the former and 1.8 million views on the latter in a week. The clip caused the game to go viral on social media. From there it snowballed fast. On May 5th, TikToker @bixtohq posted a video of himself playing the game and reacting to the song in particular, garnering over 374,000 views in three days.

Then the meme remixes started rolling in. On May 7th, 2026, X user @shagLSSJ posted a meme using "Usher Scooby Doo Party Break" where the sprites from the game are shown running through SpongeBob SquarePants scenery, writing, "scooby doo party break 2005 has been haunting me since the morning help," garnering over 7,600 likes and 1,600 reposts in a day. The "haunting me since the morning" energy is exactly what makes this whole moment so fun. The song is genuinely catchy in the most unhinged way possible, and once you hear it, it does not leave.

This is peak internet archaeology. A game with almost no cultural footprint for over a decade suddenly becomes the thing everyone is talking about, all because one streamer called it a hidden gem. Scooby-Doo Creepy Run is still playable on Kongregate right now, and with all this attention, it might be seeing the biggest player spike of its entire existence.
keyPoints: Scooby-Doo Creepy Run is a 2013 Flash game on Kongregate where Shaggy runs from a skeleton
Its soundtrack is a 2005 Usher, Lil Jon and Ludacris 'Yeah!' remix called 'Usher Scooby Doo Party Break'
Streamer RatBagTrash's April 30 clip triggered the viral moment, hitting 1.8 million TikTok views in a week
Memes and reaction videos flooded TikTok and X throughout early May 2026
The remix has existed since at least 2005, making this a 20-year-old song finally having its internet moment
